behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: In right abducens nerve palsy, diplopia is greatest:
questions.explanation: Paralysed right lateral rectus → diplopia worst looking far right, where that muscle is most needed.

questions.stem: Amblyopia:

options.A: May be the result of latent strabismus

questions.explanation: Latent strabismus can cause suppression amblyopia. It is a childhood condition, not correctable in adolescence.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a complication of thyroid ophthalmopathy?
questions.explanation: Thyroid disease causes lid retraction, not entropion. Strabismus and raised IOP do occur.

questions.stem: Sudden, painful recent decrease in visual acuity is due to:
questions.explanation: Sudden + painful + ↓VA = iritis (anterior uveitis). CRAO is painless; media opacification is gradual.

questions.stem: Metamorphopsia suggests:
questions.explanation: Distorted straight lines = macular pathology distorting photoreceptor alignment.

questions.stem: Unilateral mucous discharge and tearing in a neonate suggest:
questions.explanation: Unilateral, mucous (not purulent) → NLD obstruction. Gonococcal is purulent; chlamydial usually bilateral.

questions.stem: Chronic dacryocystitis may be complicated by:

options.C: Cellulitis of the medial canthal area

questions.explanation: All three are recognised complications of chronic sac infection.

questions.stem: Chronic conjunctivitis without mucopurulent discharge in children suggests:
questions.explanation: In children, chronic non-purulent conjunctivitis points to chlamydial (inclusion) infection.

questions.stem: Cavernous sinus thrombosis may cause:
questions.explanation: CN III involvement → parasympathetic palsy → ipsilateral mydriasis. Marcus-Gunn is an afferent (optic) defect.

questions.stem: Ectropion causes corneal damage due to:
questions.explanation: Everted lid → exposure → dryness is the primary damage mechanism. Tear overflow is a symptom; abrasion is entropion.

questions.stem: Slow and incomplete blinking suggests:
questions.explanation: Reduced, incomplete blink rate is typical of Parkinsonian/extrapyramidal disease. Lagophthalmos is failure to close, not slow blink.

questions.stem: Trauma to the free margin of the eyelid is treated with:
questions.explanation: Lid-margin trauma needs precise multilayer repair → refer to ophthalmology.

questions.stem: The greatest perceived improvement in visual acuity is from:
questions.explanation: VA is perceived logarithmically; doubling from a very low baseline (1/20→1/10) is the biggest functional gain.

questions.stem: Corneal arcus in young individuals suggests:
questions.explanation: Arcus before ~50 years → dyslipidaemia. In the elderly it is a normal aging finding.

questions.stem: Slow pain worsened by light and decreased visual acuity suggest:
questions.explanation: Photophobia + dull pain + ↓VA = iritis. Acute glaucoma is severe acute pain with a hard eye.

questions.stem: Sudden visual loss with preserved pupillary reflex suggests:
questions.explanation: Light is blocked before reaching an intact retina → pupil reflex preserved. CRAO/CRVO give an RAPD.

questions.stem: Post-septal orbital cellulitis includes:
questions.explanation: Behind the septum → orbital fat/muscle involvement → restricted motility (plus proptosis, pain on movement).

questions.stem: Bacterial conjunctivitis is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Bacterial conjunctivitis has normal VA, no significant photophobia, and mucopurulent (not filamentous) discharge.

questions.stem: Treatment of a hordeolum includes:
questions.explanation: Stye = acute infection → antibiotic drops + warm compress/lid massage. I&D is for chalazion.

questions.stem: Conjunctivitis with purulent discharge suggests:
questions.explanation: Purulent = bacterial.

questions.stem: Treatment of subconjunctival hemorrhage includes:
questions.explanation: Self-limiting — no treatment needed (artificial tears only if symptomatic dryness).

questions.stem: Oculomotor nerve palsy causes:
questions.explanation: CN III → ptosis (+ mydriasis, down-and-out eye). The deviation is DIVERGENT, so 'convergent strabismus' is wrong.

questions.stem: Slow intense pain radiating to the orbit with intact vision suggests:
questions.explanation: Autonomic orbital pain with preserved vision = cluster headache. Iritis/glaucoma reduce VA.

questions.stem: Loss of red reflex with leukocoria suggests:
questions.explanation: Among these, total cataract is the classic benign cause (retinoblastoma also causes leukocoria but isn't listed).

questions.stem: Chronic conjunctivitis without mucopurulent discharge in the elderly suggests:
questions.explanation: In the elderly, chronic non-purulent conjunctivitis → dry eye (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).

questions.stem: Herpes zoster of the first trigeminal branch begins with:
questions.explanation: Prodrome = burning + skin redness before vesicles erupt along the dermatome.

questions.stem: Ptosis covering the pupil in preschool children is treated:
questions.explanation: Visual-axis obstruction risks deprivation amblyopia → treat as soon as possible.

questions.stem: Treatment of herpes zoster ophthalmicus includes:
questions.explanation: Antivirals + topical steroids to control inflammation/keratitis.

questions.stem: Dendritic herpetic keratitis:
questions.explanation: Typically unilateral, with reduced corneal sensation (CN V), and can scar.

questions.stem: Actinic keratitis:

options.C: Is treated with anesthetic drops every 2 hours

questions.explanation: UV exposure (welding/snow). Repeated topical anaesthetics are corneal-toxic and contraindicated.

questions.stem: In adenoviral epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, the affected eye transmits the disease:

options.B: The first two days of symptoms

questions.explanation: EKC is contagious for roughly the first two weeks of symptoms.

questions.stem: Eye redness and acute pain with heavy tearing suggests:
questions.explanation: Intense surface pain + foreign-body sensation + reflex tearing = corneal abrasion.

questions.stem: Seasonal conjunctivitis beginning in spring in children suggests:
questions.explanation: Spring/seasonal onset in a child = allergic (vernal) conjunctivitis.

questions.stem: A cataract is considered mature for surgery when:
questions.explanation: Surgical timing is based on functional impairment, not a fixed VA — any of these can justify surgery.

questions.stem: Continuous occlusion of one eye in a 3-year-old child causes amblyopia after:
questions.explanation: Deprivation amblyopia develops fast in young children — roughly 3 days here.

questions.stem: Corneal hypoesthesia is associated with:
questions.explanation: Loss of protective reflexes → neurotrophic thinning → perforation.

questions.stem: The most severe postoperative complication of cataract surgery is:
questions.explanation: Endophthalmitis is a sight-threatening intraocular infection — the most feared complication.

questions.stem: Urgent indication for removal of congenital cataract is when it is:
questions.explanation: Unilateral congenital cataract carries the highest amblyopia risk → most urgent removal.

questions.stem: Neuroparalytic keratitis may be caused by:
questions.explanation: Topical anaesthetic (tetracaine) abuse destroys corneal nerve endings → loss of sensation.

questions.stem: Eye redness and deep pain increased by palpation suggest:
questions.explanation: Deep, boring, tender-to-palpation pain = scleritis.

questions.stem: Facial nerve palsy causes:
questions.explanation: CN VII → lagophthalmos/exposure, not hypoaesthesia (CN V), ptosis (CN III), or diplopia — so none of these.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with stable -3D myopia exhibits:

options.A: The image focuses behind the retina
options.D: Clear correction with biconvex converging lenses

questions.explanation: Myopia focuses light in front of the retina → blurred distance vision; corrected with biconcave lenses.

questions.stem: Gradual painless decrease in visual acuity is due to:
questions.explanation: Gradual + painless + ↓VA = media opacification (typically cataract).

questions.stem: Definitive treatment of chalazion is:
questions.explanation: A persistent chalazion is definitively managed by incision & curettage.

questions.stem: Pterygium in a 57-year-old covering part of the cornea:

options.B: Develops on the temporal side of the conjunctiva
options.C: Causes conjunctival redness and mucopurulent discharge
options.D: Reduces visual acuity due to induced astigmatism

questions.explanation: A pterygium encroaching on the cornea induces astigmatism → ↓VA. It grows nasally, not temporally.

questions.stem: Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis is characterized by:

options.A: May lead to keratitis requiring topical steroids
options.B: Purulent discharge and foreign body sensation when blinking
options.C: Need for topical and systemic treatment

questions.explanation: Hallmarks are itching and watery discharge. (Vernal disease is the severe variant that may need steroids.)

questions.stem: Myopia: which is correct?

options.A: A myope with -3D sees clearly at 33 cm without accommodation
options.C: It is the opposite of presbyopia

questions.explanation: Far point of −3D = 33 cm. Myopia uses biconcave lenses and is the opposite of hyperopia (not presbyopia).

questions.stem: In ischemic central retinal vein occlusion:

options.B: Sudden vision loss with marked retinal vessel thinning
options.D: Sudden unilateral visual loss ≤1/20 with venous congestion, widespread hemorrhages, and cotton-wool exudates

questions.explanation: Ischaemic CRVO: profound sudden loss with engorged veins, diffuse haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots. Rubeosis is a harmful complication.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old with -2D myopia sees clearly without correction:
questions.explanation: Far point of −2D = 1/2 m = 50 cm.

questions.stem: Slow visceral eye pain with photophobia is relieved by:
questions.explanation: Iritis pain is from ciliary spasm → relieved by cycloplegic/parasympatholytic drops (atropine).

questions.stem: In right trochlear nerve palsy, diplopia is greatest in:
questions.explanation: Right superior oblique depresses the adducted eye → diplopia worst looking down and to the left.

questions.stem: Sudden diplopia in a 75-year-old is due to:
questions.explanation: Microvascular CN VI palsy is the commonest cause of isolated diplopia in the elderly.

questions.stem: Sudden painless recent visual acuity loss is due to:
questions.explanation: Sudden + painless + severe = CRAO.

questions.stem: A lesion of the entire thickness of the occipital lobe causes:
questions.explanation: Destroying one visual cortex → contralateral homonymous hemianopia.

questions.stem: Which is correct about optic disc edema?

options.A: In papilledema, congested arteries are observed
options.B: Ischemic edema causes significant and sudden vision loss
options.C: It is not associated with intracranial hypertension
options.D: Papilledema is characterized by decreased vision in early stages

questions.explanation: AION causes sudden marked vision loss. Papilloedema congests veins and spares VA early.

questions.stem: Age-related macular degeneration: which is correct?
questions.explanation: AMD: usually bilateral, normal IOP, gradual central VA loss (dry form).

questions.stem: In temporal arteritis, we find:
questions.explanation: GCA: jaw claudication AND risk of blindness (AION) — both true.

questions.stem: A scotoma in the upper visual field is due to:
questions.explanation: Inferior nerve fibres are damaged first in glaucoma → superior arcuate (upper field) scotoma.

questions.stem: A welding worker presents at night to the ER with:

options.A: Conjunctival redness and central corneal ulcer
options.C: Treated with prolonged topical anesthetic and antibiotic drops
options.D: Intense pain and tearing with decreased vision

questions.explanation: UV photokeratitis: delayed intense pain, tearing, ↓VA. Prolonged anaesthetics are contraindicated.

questions.stem: In thyroid eye disease with compressive optic neuropathy, which is true:

options.A: The pupil is miotic and unresponsive to light
options.B: The pupil is normal but visual acuity is reduced
options.C: Reduced visual acuity, color perception, and pupillary reflexes
options.D: The contralateral pupil does not respond to light

questions.explanation: Optic nerve compression → ↓VA, ↓colour vision, and abnormal pupil reflexes (RAPD).
